"Laguna Beach," which debuted on MTV in 2004, introduced viewers to to a group of real-life high school students in Laguna Beach, California.

For the show's reunion, which premiered April 10 on Roku, Bosworth Natale met up with former cast member Lauren Conrad to shoot scenes inside the offices of Bosworth Natale's women's wellness company, Love Wellness.

Bosworth Natale also joined Conrad, Stephen Colletti, Kristin Cavallari and other cast members over video to reminisce about their teenage dramas, which included no shortage of platonic dustups and romances.
"I think everybody was very nervous, actually, to get together but it turned out to be very healing," said Bosworth Natale. "Everyone keeps saying, oh, we feel so good after doing the reunion."
"It was actually really great. I hadn't seen a lot of the people in many, many years," she added.
The reunion was especially meaningful for Bosworth Natale, who missed her "real" high school reunion because she was at home doing "an IVF round."
"So, it was a true reunion for me to be able to get together with everybody," she noted.

During her visit to TODAY, Bosworth Natale also recounted how she and Conrad initially caught the attention of the series' producers, who were touring their high school's area in search of potential cast members.
She and Conrad, she recalled, were wearing “matching Juicy tracks suits” and sporting similar “crazy blond hair."
"Lauren and I were actually ditching class and we had to walk past the (administration) office to get to our car to go to wherever it was that we were going," she said.
"I accidentally set off my car alarm in front of the office as the producers were walking up the street," she said, laughing.
"It just was fate, I suppose," she added.
Being a star on "Laguna Beach" turned out to be a "life-changing' experience, she said.
"So many of us have had such success as a result of the show with different businesses and amazing families," she said. "It's just very heartwarming for me to go back and see the reunion."
"It's a beautiful time capsule for everybody," she added.
Welcoming her first child earlier this year so "fundamentally" changed Bosworth Natale, she decided to launch her own motherhood podcast "Tell Me I’m a Good Mom."
"Moms are tough and moms are great and moms deserve their own stage to be applauded and that's what I'm doing with the new podcast," she said.
Bosworth Natale announced last month on Instagram that she didn't attend the "Laguna Beach" reunion in person because she'd become a new mom.

“I didn’t go!!!!,” she wrote. “But not bc I didn’t want to — I did! I live in NYC and I just had a babbbyyyy and I have to have a fragment of placenta removed in a few days. I wore my black and white outfit at home yesterday in solidarity lol.”

title: Where ‘Euphoria’ Left All Its Main Characters Off After Season 2
summary: "Euphoria" Season 3 premieres Sunday, April 12, on HBO. Here's where Season 2 left Rue, Jules, Maddy, Nate, Cassie and more.
story: “Guilty or innocent?” asks "Euphoria's" Season 1 closing song, “All For Us,” by Zendaya and Labrinth — a question still hanging over its protagonists.
It’s been four years since the show's neon hues and glitter makeup last lit up our screens. Season 3 premieres Sunday, April 12, on HBO, reuniting viewers with East Highland’s chaos-prone cohort.
Season 3's trailer previews a significant time jump and flashes of what’s ahead: Rue in Mexico, Cassie in a wedding gown, Jules in New York, Lexi in Hollywood. But how did we get here?
Before we reunite with this tangled web of toxic friendships and fractured relationships, here’s where we left off.
Rue’s Relapse

At the center of the show is Rue, played by Zendaya, and her struggle with drug addiction. Rue’s sobriety was always fragile, but Season 2 shattered any illusion of stability. After relapsing, initially using at a party with Dominic Fike’s character, Elliot, she spiraled into one of the show’s most harrowing arcs, burning nearly every bridge in her life.

Most ominously, there’s Laurie (Martha Kelly), the drug dealer who fronted Rue a suitcase of drugs with the expectation she’d sell them and repay the debt, with interest. Unfortunately for Rue, her mom flushed the stash during an intervention, leaving Rue with nothing to pay Laurie back.
With Season 3's trailer placing Rue in Mexico, it’s hard not to wonder if she’s still trying to outrun or finally settle that debt.
Cal Jacobs’ Arrest
Cal Jacobs is played by Eric Dane, who filmed Season 3 amid his diagnosis with ALS before his death in February at age 53.

Cal, the father of Jacob Elordi's Nate, spent years living a carefully concealed double life, secretly recording his sexual encounters with men and transgender women while repressing his own identity. That facade finally cracked in a drunken, unfiltered confession to his family that exposed not just his actions, but his true self.

At the end of Season 2, Cal was arrested after Nate turned him into the police. But that doesn’t neatly close the chapter. His relationship with Nate is in ruins, and his history with Jules (Hunter Schafer) lingers in ways that are difficult to untangle.
And if the Season 3 trailer is any indication, his history with Jules is far from settled.
Lexi’s Play

After being sidelined most of Season 1, Maude Apatow’s character, Lexi, stepped into her own voice in the most recent season. She wrote, directed and performed a school play drawn directly from her and her sister Cassie's (Sydney Sweeney) lives, staging thinly veiled versions of their friends' most private moments for an audience of their classmates.

For the people being portrayed onstage, it wasn’t hard to recognize themselves. Rue saw a version of herself struggle with drugs through Lexi’s empathetic gaze, while Cassie saw something far less forgiving, fueling an explosive onstage fight between the sisters. What Lexi framed as honesty landed, for many involved, as exposure.
Ashtray's Death

Fezco (Angus Cloud) experienced tragedy at the very end of "Euphoria" Season 2. A police raid of the drug dealer's house resulted in his pseudo little brother Ashtray’s (Javon “Wanna” Walton) death and Fez’s arrest, cutting short one of the show’s most unexpectedly tender arcs: his connection with Lexi.
Angus Cloud died in 2023 at age 25 of an accidental overdose. His character Fez’s story now stands as one of the show’s most unresolved.
The 'Euphoria' Love Triangles
In addition to plenty of plot twists and action, "Euphoria" also features a complex web of romances and hookups. Here's where Season 2 left off these relationships:

Perhaps the most explosive fallout from Lexi’s play was the physical altercation between Maddy (Alexa Demie) and her best friend Cassie, though the fuse had already been lit. Rue accidentally exposed Cassie's relationship with Maddy's ex-boyfriend Nate in the penultimate episode, and by the time Lexi’s play aired it out for all to see, the finale's confrontation was inevitable.

And yet, Season 3's trailer complicates everything: Cassie and Nate appear to make it all the way to the altar, while Maddy somehow finds her way back into Cassie’s orbit. If anything, it suggests the triangle isn’t resolved so much as reshaped.

Rue and Jules’ relationship has always been defined by intensity and instability, but the introduction of Elliot in Season 2 complicated things further. Rue relapsed and hid it from Jules, often using with Elliot behind her back, while Elliot inserted himself into their dynamic in ways that blurred emotional and physical boundaries.
The breaking point came when Elliot told Jules about Rue’s drug use, which was portrayed by her friends as an intervention but seen by Rue as betrayal.
By the end of the season, trust was broken on all sides. The intervention set off Rue so much, she broke up with Jules.
"You and me, we're f---ing done. There's nothing f---ing there anymore," Rue yelled at a crying Jules.
Elliot is left in the middle, both a catalyst for the pair's fallout and a casualty of it.
Jules’ entanglement with both Nate and Cal sits at the intersection of some of "Euphoria’s" darkest themes: control, exploitation, and secrecy.
Jules and Cal first crossed paths on a hookup app, leading to a clandestine encounter Cal secretly recorded. One of many he documented without his partners' knowledge.

That history would later collide when Nate manipulates Jules — catfishing her and blackmailing her with the footage — and turns his father's secrets into leverage. Even with Cal arrested, the psychological grip of this triangle doesn’t disappear overnight.

title: Amanda Batula Apologizes to 'Everyone I've Disappointed,' Confirms She'll Attend 'Summer House' Reunion
summary: "Summer House" star Amanda Batula is issuing a sweeping apology to "everyone" she's "disappointed and hurt" following confirmation that she and co-star West Wilson are dating."Hi guys, I’ll keep this short and sweet.
story: "Summer House" star Amanda Batula is issuing a sweeping apology to "everyone" she's "disappointed and hurt" following confirmation that she and co-star West Wilson are dating.

"Hi guys, I’ll keep this short and sweet. I’ve obviously been MIA, but I wanted to come back on and say that I’m truly sorry to everyone I’ve disappointed and hurt, especially those I know personally who I’ve reached out to individually," Batula wrote April 10 in a statement in her Instagram story.

“For the sake of my mental health,” she continued, “I’m going to try to start living life with some sense of normalcy. If you see me out or posting online, please know that this still weighs very heavily on me. I’m not ignoring what’s happened or what’s unfolded.”
Batula added that she would attend the show's upcoming Season 10 reunion, where she would be “addressing any and all questions honestly and directly there.”
Batula and Wilson shocked fans when they confirmed their romance in a joint statement March 31, a little over two months after Batula announced her split from estranged husband Kyle Cooke in January.
Batula's close friend and co-star, Ciara Miller, was previously in an on-and-off romance with Wilson.
"We’ve seen the growing online speculation, so while this is still very new, we wanted to provide some clarity,” read the statement, which both Batula and Wilson shared on social media.

“It was never our intention to purposely hide anything. Given the complicated relationship dynamics involved and the scrutiny that comes with being on a reality show, we needed a little space to process things privately before speaking on it,” they continued.
The couple went on to say their “connection” grew out of a “genuine, longstanding friendship.”
Miller recently posed a pointed question about hooking up with a friend's ex-boyfriend while interviewing Sydney Sweeney at the “Euphoria” Season 3 premiere on April 7.
“I mean, if you steal your friend’s ex-boyfriend ...” Miller asked Sweeney on the red carpet.
“It’s not good,” Sweeney said.

(Granted, Sweeney is involved in a a similar storyline on “Euphoria,” which returns to HBO Max on April 12. Sweeney’s character, Cassie, secretly hooked up with Nate (Jacob Elordi), the ex of her best friend Maddy (Alexa Demie), in Season 2 of the drama.)

At the "Euphoria" premiere, Miller went on to ask Sweeney, “What do you think is the next step? I feel like you ought to get married,” seemingly referencing the Season 3 trailer for “Euphoria," which shows Cassie walking down the aisle.
“I mean, I would hope so. You got to make it worth it,” Sweeney responded.
“You got to make it worth it,” Miller said, before turning and looking directly at the camera.
Cooke also addressed his ex Batula's new romance with Wilson during an April 1 interview for the “So Bad It’s Good With Ryan Bailey” podcast.
When host Ryan Bailey asked Cooke how he was doing, the reality star responded, “I’m good. You know, I appreciate people checking in. I’m actually really worried about Amanda if I’m being honest.”
Cooke said he'd spoken with Batula, whom he said had "mental health issues," on the nights before and after she and Wilson released their statements confirming their romance.
"I understand people have all sorts of opinions, and I’m not justifying any behavior. But like, from what I’m seeing, she’s kind of getting cyber bullied,” Cooke said.

Cooke went on to say Wilson "seems to be the kind of guy playing multiple women at the same time." He added Wilson had reached out to him in  “some half-a---ed text message" that he compared to a "word salad."
Cooke added that he was now questioning the timeline of Batula and Wilson's romance.

“It definitely makes me second guess a lot of things last summer, at a minimum," he said. "Just how checked out she was. In my own unsuccessful ways, I was still trying to put an effort in. I mean, it’s a mental mind f--- for me.”
"I’m still trying to figure out the details. But I know I can imagine West is using the same charm that he’s used on just about every other chick," he added.

title: 'Laguna Beach' Cast Now: What Happened After the MTV Show Ended?
summary: Where is the Laguna Beach cast now? They're reuniting for a reunion on April 10. Read on for an update about their lives now.
story: Nearly 20 years after "Laguna Beach" aired its last episode, the cast of the MTV reality series is getting back together for a reunion.
On April 10, "The Reunion: Laguna Beach" will air on the Roku Channel.

Several members of the original cast will appear in the reunion special, including Lauren Conrad, Stephen Colletti, Kristin Cavallari, Trey Phillips, Talan Torriero, Christina Schuller, Lo Bosworth, Dieter Schmitz, Jessica Smith, Alex Hooser and Morgan Olsen.
Before the reunion premieres, we're sharing an update on what some of the cast members have been up to over the last two decades.
Kristin Cavallari
"Laguna Beach" viewers were always Team Lauren or Team Kristin in the Kristin-Lauren-Stephen love triangle.
In 2022, Kristin Cavallari and Stephen Colletti opened up about their enduring friendship in an episode of their “Laguna Beach” rewatch podcast, “Back to the Beach with Kristin and Stephen.”
“We were great. I mean, I would say you were my first love. Like, we really had a very sweet, great relationship,” Cavallari said.
In the episode, Cavallari revealed that she and Colletti have "stayed in touch over the years" and said they "reconnected a little bit more in the last year and a half."

After "Laguna Beach" ended, Cavallari appeared on 24 episodes of the show's spinoff series, "The Hills," per IMDB. Later, she also starred in an E! reality series called “Very Cavallari” and a docuseries called "Honestly Cavallari: The Headline Tour."

Cavallari has made guest appearances in several scripted TV series over the years, including "9-1-1:Nashville," "The League," "The Middle" and "CSI: NY." She also competed in Season 13 of "Dancing With the Stars."
In 2016, Cavallari published a book titled, "Balancing in Heels: My Journey to Health, Happiness, and Making It All Work." She's also the author of three cookbooks.
Cavallari married football player Jay Cutler in 2013. The couple went on to have three children. In 2020, the couple announced that they were getting a divorce.
These days, Cavallari keeps busy with her podcast "Let’s Be Honest with Kristin Cavallari."
In 2025, Cavallari appeared at the Shop TODAY Stages event and spoke about her work as an entrepreneur and her successful lifestyle brand, Uncommon James.
Lauren Conrad

"Laguna Beach" may have pit Lauren Conrad and Kristin Cavallari against each other, but the former rivals cleared the air in 2022 during an episode of the “Back to the Beach with Kristin and Stephen” podcast.

“My take on it is, you and I really never had any beef, really” Cavallari told Conrad. “Obviously, there was a little truth to what happened with the three of us. I felt like MTV coming kept it alive and made it way worse than it ever would have been.”
“Yeah, I think it was done when they started filming,” Conrad said.

Later on, Cavallari said they "never had an issue" with each other, but Conrad said, “I mean, we had an issue with each other. But it had been squashed. They ran with it. It made for an interesting show.”
They spoke more about their feud in an interview with TODAY.com ahead of the premiere.
“Obviously, it’s been so long. I mean, I’m just happy that we’ve gotten this time together where we can truly just put everything in the past. It’s been really nice,” Cavallari said.

After the end of "Laguna Beach," Conrad starred in the spinoff series, "The Hills," which followed her as she attended FIT and pursued a career in the fashion industry. The show also highlighted her internships with Teen Vogue and People's Revolution.

Today, Conrad is the designer behind the womenswear line LC Lauren Conrad and the children's line Little Co. In 2013, Conrad and her friend Hannah Skvarla founded a nonprofit online fair trade shop called The Little Market.
In 2014, Conrad married former musician, William Tell, who was part of the band Something Corporate and now works as a lawyer. The couple went on to welcome two sons.
Lo Bosworth

During her "Laguna Beach" days, Lo Bosworth was pretty tight with Lauren Conrad. In 2024, Bosworth told E! News she was still in touch with her high school pal but said she's “not in constant touch with a ton of people from the show.”

In a recent post on her Instagram page, Bosworth reflected on her time on the reality series, writing, "I was 16, had no idea what I was doing, and somehow that became the start of a semi public life that I’ve been navigating ever since."
After wrapping up "Laguna Beach," Bosworth appeared in the show's spinoff series, "The Hills."

Bosworth attended the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) then moved to New York City, where she started a cooking and lifestyle blog. In a recent Instagram post, she explained that she had "severe vitamin deficiencies and was seeking answers" at the time.
Bosworth later created Love Wellness, a women's wellness brand that's focused on vaginal health, hormones, gut health and mental health.
"Took about 10 years, a lot of tears, and more product development meetings than I can count. I love my job!!!" she wrote on Instagram.
In July 2025, Bosworth married businessman Dom Natale. The couple welcomed their first child together, a daughter named Nelle, in January 2026.
Bosworth currently has a podcast called "Tell Me I'm a Good Mom" and a Substack newsletter about wellness.
Stephen Colletti
Stephen Colletti was known as the heartthrob of the group and was involved in a love triangle with Conrad and Cavallari during his "Laguna Beach" years.
In a recent interview with Entertainment Tonight, Colletti reflected on some of his romantic escapades from his younger years.
“I made some decisions back in the day that I’m not proud of and and we’ve had a lot of growth since then,” he said.
Overall, Colletti said the show’s producers “took their liberties” and dramatized the love triangle aspect of the show.

"When it comes to the love triangle, from the jump, they made it seem like Lauren and I were just straight up having this affair out in the open when I never cheated on Kristen during our relationship. Lauren and I never did anything like that," he said.

Following his time on "Laguna Beach," Colletti starred as Chase in "One Tree Hill" and appeared in 57 episodes. Since then, he's built up several other acting credits in projects like the TV series "Everyone Is Doing Great," the TV movie "Hometown Christmas" and several others.
Colletti also appeared in Season 4 of the Peacock reality series "Traitors."
In his personal life, Colletti is currently expecting his first child with his wife, NASCAR host and reporter Alex Weaver. The couple got married in October 2025.
Morgan Smith
After "Laguna Beach," Morgan Smith (formerly Olsen) pursued a career in the fashion industry like several of her former co-stars.
Smith, who now lives in South Carolina with her husband and three children, has a clothing brand named minnow that makes clothes for women, men and children.
The brand's website says minnow is "a reflection of the life and style of its founder, Morgan Smith, a mother and entrepreneur with a background in premium product marketing."
Per the website, Smith previously worked in both Los Angeles and New York.
Christina Sinclair
Christina Sinclair (formerly Schuller) spoke to Time in 2014 to discuss the experience of signing up for "Laguna Beach."

"We’re sitting there in class, and over the daily announcements they announce, ‘OK everyone, MTV’s here. They’re going to cast a reality show on Laguna Beach. If you’re interested, sign up in the quad at break.’ And I think for any 18 year old, that’s a dream come true," she said.
After "Laguna Beach," she attended the University of Southern California and moved back to her hometown to teach fitness classes.
Per Sinclair’s website, she works as a certified fitness instructor who specializes in barre fitness. In 2012, she was ranked a top 20 barre instructor in Los Angeles.
Sinclair is married to her husband, Chad, and the couple has three children.
Jason Wahler
Jason Wahler, who was known for his rocky relationship with Conrad, didn't have a stellar reputation when he appeared on "Laguna Beach" and "The Hills."
While struggling with substance abuse, Wahler appeared on “Celebrity Rehab with Dr. Drew” in 2010.
In 2016, Wahler opened up to Entertainment Tonight about his addiction journey, noting that he particularly struggled with alcohol and cocaine.
"I became a totally different person active in my addiction versus when I’m sober and living life in recovery," he said.
Wahler was arrested several times and sought treatment at multiple rehab centers over the years. He told Entertainment Tonight he was inspired to really change his life after attempting suicide.
"My addiction drove me to suicide," he said. "Not contemplation -- actually attempting suicide -- and somebody found me and that’s why I’m still here today. I’m very grateful for that."
Wahler married his girlfriend Ashley Slack in 2013. The couple now has three children together.
Since starting his recovery journey, Wahler has been transparent about the highs and lows he's encountered over time, including a relapse in 2018.

Wahler, who appeared on "The Hills: New Beginnings," describes himself as a "passionate advocate for addiction education and prevention" on his website. He works as a guest speaker and recovery advocate.
Wahler is currently the director of the Change Your Brain Foundation and is on the board of Cure Addiction Now.
Trey Phillips
Along with several of his classmates, Trey Phillips appeared on "Laguna Beach" as a high school student.
When Phillips was a guest on Colletti and Cavallari's podcast in 2022, fans of "Laguna Beach" learned that he's kept in touch with Colletti since filming wrapped many years ago.
Cavallari also complimented Phillips on how put together he was while they were starring in the show, saying, "You were so mature and so wise beyond your years."

After high school, Phillips attended Bard College to study human rights. He later dropped out to pursue a career in fashion and tried to create his own hat and sunglasses companies. When those failed, he went on to study fashion and later scored a job as a designer for Vera Wang, a company he worked at for seven years.
Later, Phillips relocated to London to continue his fashion career.
Talan Torriero
In "Laguna Beach," Talan Torriero was known as a bit of a Casanova and was romantically linked to Cavallari at times. But he told E! News in 2022 that he was really a "hopeless romantic."
"I loved being in relationships," he said.
In a recent interview with Entertainment Tonight, Torriero confirmed that he dated singer Nicole Scherzinger in the early 2000s.
"She was a big part of my life. I’m really happy to see all the success she’s had," he said.
Torriero got married to his wife Danielle in 2014. The couple shares three children.

"I think that young Talon had a good time. He had a lot of fun. Talon today is boring. He’s married. He’s got three kids. He’s got basketball practice on Saturday, so he’s got to get home tomorrow," he recently told Entertainment Tonight.
Dieter Schmitz
In a recent interview with Entertainment Tonight, Schmitz described what made the reality show so popular, saying the group had been friends since the first grade.
“I think that’s what made the show special,” he said.
Looking back on his experience of filming a reality show, Schmitz joked that he has one regret.
"I said a lot of bad words and they didn't use beeps, so I would just go quiet. And my mom was like, 'Your mic always goes out,'" he teased.
Life has changed a lot for Dieter Schmitz since his "Laguna Beach" days. On his Instagram page, the former reality star describes himself as a "proud papa of two," a "husband" and a "hotelier."
When Schmitz got married in 2016, Colletti served as a groomsman.
During his interview with Entertainment Tonight, Schmitz said he typically doesn't bring up his time on reality TV at work, but noted that the reunion had "brought it back a little bit."
"All my coworkers that I hadn’t told, they’re like, 'What is happening?'" he said.
Jessica Evans

Jessica Evans (formerly Smith) appears in the "Laguna Beach" reunion special. During an interview with Entertainment Tonight, the former reality star said she still keeps in touch with Cavallari, Torriero and Olsen.
“We’ve got our little fab four group chat that we text on all the time so that’s great. We’re all very close. We were all in the same graduating class as well,” she said.
Evans, who has been married almost 17 years, said her husband hasn't watched "Laguna Beach."
“He’s just not a reality TV guy,” she said.
However, a few of her four children may be interested in seeing the show.

"I told my kids that they could watch it when they are the age that I was when I was on it. So my oldest is 15, which is how old I was. He has no interest. My 13-year-old, who is here with me right now, enlightens me today that she’s watched Season 1 at the neighbor’s house. I was like, 'Great. Well, better than Season 2, right?' I was like, 'Don’t go into Season 2 yet. Wait for me for that,'" she said.
Alex Hooser

Fans of "Laguna Beach" will recall an Alex Hooser appearing on the show. Now, she goes by Lauren Olsen (her married name). Her birth name was Lauren Alexandria Hooser, as she explained in a recent Instagram video.
"I've always been Lauren. Up until about third grade is when I changed to being Alex or Alexandria, my middle name," she said.
As it turns out, her family moved to Laguna Beach during the summer of third grade and a young Lauren decided it was time for a change.
"As we're registering for school, I asked my parents if I could go by my middle name, Alexandria," she said.
Olsen captioned her post, "Lauren, Alex, Lauren Olsen, call me whatever you want at this point 😂."
In a recent interview with Entertainment Tonight, Olsen also addressed her name change.
"My real name is Lauren, which I kind of like to go back to. I feel like Hannah Montana. I can be like, 'This is my Hannah.' And then back home, I’m Lauren," she said.
Olsen was always close to Cavallari during filming, and she said they have "maintained" their friendship.

"Kristen is the real deal when it comes to a best friend. And I’m forever grateful for that friendship. And even though we haven’t necessarily seen each other throughout the years, we’ve been pretty good at staying in touch. And I love that girl, man. She’s ride or die till the end. And so am I," she said.
After "Laguna Beach" ended, Olsen lived in Los Angeles for a while before moving back to Laguna Beach.

Olsen is now married and has two children. She and her husband lived in Hawaii for around 12 years before relocating to California to be closer to family, as she revealed in an episode of Cavallari and Colletti's podcast in 2022.

title: Is it Really Possible to Watch Coachella at Home? Yes — Here’s How
summary: Here's how you can stream the performances at Coachella.
story: If you can’t make it to Coachella, don’t worry — Coachella can come to you.

The 25th edition of the famed Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, which takes place at the Empire Polo Club in Indio, California, kicks off April 10. A wide range of stars will perform, including Justin Bieber, Sabrina Carpenter, Teddy Swims and Karol G.
The event runs over two weekends. The first is April 10, 11 and 12, followed by April 17, 18 and 19.

This year’s event will also be available to watch at home, so if you don’t plan to trek to the desert, you can slip into your jammies, fire up your computer or phone and still take in what the festival’s musical acts have to offer.
How to Watch Coachella At Home For Free
The festival’s performances will be available to stream on YouTube.
Head to Coachella’s page and scroll to the stage you’d like to watch, or click here for a playlist of all the available livestreams, which are set to start at 4 p.m. PT on April 10.
In addition to the main stage, or the Coachella Stage, shows will be held on seven other stages: Outdoor Theatre, Sahara, Mojave, Gobi, Sonora and Quasar.
Here’s the Full Coachella Schedule
There are some real heavyweights who will take the main Coachella stage. (All times are PT.)
Sabrina Carpenter will perform from 9:05 p.m. until 10:35 p.m. on April 10. She will be followed at midnight by electronic artist Anyma. Teddy Swims will perform earlier in the night at 5:30 p.m.
Bieber will headline on April 11 with an 11:25 p.m. show. That will wrap up a night that features performances from other acts such as The Strokes at 9 p.m. and Addison Rae at 5:30 p.m.
The final night on April 12 will culminate with Karol G’s 9:55 p.m. set. Young Thug will perform before her at 7:50 p.m.
The whole livestream schedule is available here.

You can check the entire Coachella schedule for a list of all the talent performing, as well as what stage they’ll be on and what time their sets begin. More highlights include Katseye at 8 p.m. Friday on the Sahara stage, Sombr at 7:05 p.m. Saturday at the Outdoor Theatre, FKA Twigs at 8:45 p.m. Sunday on the Mojave stage and more.
The schedule for the second weekend is not yet available.

title: The 4 Books Harlan Coben Is Reading and Loving Lately
summary: Bestselling author Harlan Coben stopped by TODAY on April 10 to share the books he's reading and recommending this month
story: Bestselling author Harlan Coben stopped by TODAY on April 10 to share the books he's reading and recommending this month.

The author also has exciting news: He's writing a memoir! "Plot Twist: Life, Craft, and the Messy First Draft," out in September, tracks Coben's life as a prolific writer. Coben has written 35 books and counting and has sold over 100 million copies.
"I wrote a how-to book. I want to inspire you to write, create or paint. All the things I've learned, I'm passing them along," he said on TODAY.
Below, he shares the books he's reading and loving lately.
Best Mystery
Mad Mabel by Sally Hepworth
Mad Mabel is a funny, inspiring, story of an underestimated woman about to prove everyone wrong.
At 15, Elsie Fitzpatrick became Australia's youngest convicted murderer. Now 81, Elsie has left that behind her — until a young girl moves in across the road and unearths her past.
"Sally Hepworth is a huge star in Australia. This is the book that's going to make her a huge star here," Coben said on TODAY. He calls this book "funny, twisty and suspenseful."
Best Contemporary Read
The Left And The Lucky by Willy Vlautin
This text is an honest display of the power of friendship and how, in various ways, it can save our lives.

"The Left and the Lucky" is the latest from an author who is known for his portraits of contemporary American life. In this one, a young boy befriends his next-door neighbor, offering him an escape from his tumultuous family life.
Coben calls this book "gut-wrenching" and "compassionate," with shades of John Steinbeck. There's "dignity and integrity" in this working-class story, he says.
Best Family Drama
This Is Not About Us by Allegra Goodman
Filled with humor, this is a story about growing old and the love that connects a family across generations.

How is it possible that an apple pie could cause a years-long rift between sisters? After Sylvia and Helen Rubinstein's sister passes away, their relationship is in turmoil, enough so that a baked good could cause a major problem.
Coben calls this his "favorite novel of the year" so far.
What Harlan's Reading
Ghost Town by Tom Perrotta
Ghost Town illuminates how the past haunts the present, even when we think we’ve left it behind.
For his 11th novel, Tom Perrotta returned to the New Jersey of his youth to write a story of a teenager whose life spurs into a different, and somewhat supernatural, direction after extreme grief.
"You'll love every page," Coben said.

title: Noah Kahan Shares the Advice From His Therapist He's 'Never Been Able to Do'
summary: Noah Kahan revealed that he fears he's already peaked in life during a candid conversation from his documentary "Out of Body," which premieres on Netflix April 13
story: Noah Kahan revealed that he fears he's already peaked in life during a candid conversation from his documentary "Out of Body," which premieres on Netflix April 13.

Kahan, the singer songwriter who skyrocketed to fame with the 2022 album "Stick Season," opened up about his complicated feelings surrounding his hometown, his success and his mental health in a new clip from the documentary, which aired exclusively on TODAY April 10.
"When I was in Hanover, Strafford, I felt suffocated by it," Kahan said. "And then when I left, I yearned for like, its simplicity."
"You can't just escape who you are because you moved to a different place," he added.
Speaking with his brother while looking at the New York City skyline, Kahan was asked if he worries that he's peaked or will peak.
"Uh yeah, yes I do," he answered. "All the time. That's all I think about, really."

"You know, tomorrow if I don't play a great show at MSG like, what could that mean? That could mean that people (start) losing interest, and the fans start to think it's boring and stale, and the label loses faith in me."
He then summarized advice he's received from a therapist: "Failure is part of it, and you need to prepare yourself for that, because it'll let you accept the moment you're in."
"I just have never really been able to do that," he added.

Kahan debuted on the music season with the 2019 album "Busyhead." He went on to release an EP "Cape Elizabeth" in 2020 followed by "I Was / I Am" in 2021. But it was "Stick Season," which features hits like "Northern Attitude," "Homesick" and "Dial Drunk," that made him a household name.
Kahan is set to release his first follow-up to that album, "The Great Divide," on April 24, and he's set to go back out on tour in June.
The upcoming Netflix doc will feature footage from a year and a half in Kahan's life, he revealed in an Instagram post.

"It was weird, difficult, and genuinely beautiful to experience," he wrote. "This documentary captures so much more than I ever thought the world would see about myself, my creative journey and my family. I am nervous about it because it’s personal and some things I touch on are painful to think about and be reminded of. I remember though that anything scary or hard is usually worth sharing with the world."

title: TODAY Makeover Gives 1 Mom a Dramatic Hair Refresh: ‘I Feel Like an ‘It’ Girl’
summary: In a TODAY Show makeover segment with Jenna Lyons and Chris Appleton, one mom gets a hair transformation with a platinum blond bob.
story: One New York mom of two is feeling like an “It” girl after her Pop-Up Makeover on TODAY.

On the April 9 episode of TODAY with Jenna & Sheinelle, the two co-hosts were joined by celebrity hairstylist Chris Appleton and fashion businesswoman Jenna Lyons, who introduced their latest makeover nominee.

For a Pop-Up Makeover, Appleton and Lyons surprise one deserving viewer with a hair and style transformation. In this segment, Appleton and Lyons visited an unsuspecting mom named Courtney in Astoria, New York, and revealed that her sister-in-law Tanjila nominated her for a makeover.
Tanjila wrote to the duo that she wanted Courtney, a 39-year-old administrator at New York University, to experience the transformation because she “prioritizes everyone but herself.”

Courtney spent the last 10 years focusing on her career and marriage while raising her two children and renovating her home. Last year, she also decided to start her fitness journey. So, she was in need of an updated look and wardrobe to match the new her.
When Appleton and Lyons found Courtney and Tanjila in New York, the administrator was shocked to see them.

“I’m a mom, so I haven’t had time to get my hair done and really have a day where I feel confident and beautiful,” Courtney told Appleton and Lyons. “I’m so excited! I can’t believe this is happening. This is crazy!”
Appleton and Lyons then whisked Courtney away for her day of full glam.
Tanjila and Courtney’s best friend Maria joined Jenna Bush Hager, Sheinelle Jones, Lyons and Appleton in the studio for the mom’s grand reveal.

Courtney strutted out, debuting her platinum blond bob. She had a pep in her step as she twirled to model her Aritzia crescendo sweater, Aritzia Henrietta midi black satin skirt with white lace trim and a Sam Edelman oversized black leather clutch. A pair of Sam Edelman black strappy kitten heels completed her look.
“Oh my God, you look amazing!” Tanjila told her sister-in-law.
Maria chimed in and said, “My hands are shaking! Wow, you look so beautiful.”
Courtney was also a big fan of her makeover.
“I love it. I feel so confident, so beautiful. I feel like an ‘It’ girl in New York City, and I always felt like that on the inside,” she said. “It was amazing. Thank you guys so much.”

Jenna applauded her look, particularly her shorter, lighter do. Appleton mentioned that the style is trending right now thanks to Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy, adding that Courtney’s cut is a “quiet luxury bob.”
He also said the cut has versatility, allowing Courtney to wear it straight or wavy, and that the color makes her blue eyes pop.
“Out with the old and in with the new, and she fully embraced it,” Appleton said.
As for her styling, Lyons said she appreciated that Courtney was open to try something new.
She recalled Courtney saying after seeing the clothes picked out for her, “‘I would never have pulled any of this. Let’s try it all on.’”
Lyons wanted to choose items that could be dressed up or down depending on the day.
Courtney said she was looking forward to wearing her outfit for a date night with her husband. At the end of the segment, she hugged her sister-in-law and friend and thanked them again.

title: U.S. and Iran could hold new peace talks as soon as this week, sources say
summary: A new round of in-person talks between the United States and Iran could be held as early as this week, two people familiar with the ongoing negotiations told NBC News
story: A new round of in-person talks between the United States and Iran could be held as early as this week, two people familiar with the ongoing negotiations told NBC News.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

It comes after talks led by Vice President JD Vance in Pakistan over the weekend failed to reach a peace agreement to end the war. Both Vance and President Donald Trump offered hope for future talks Monday, even as U.S. forces launched a blockade barring ships from entering or exiting Iranian ports.

Opening the Strait of Hormuz  for a free flow of shipping is a major sticking point in the negotiations for any agreement, one of the people said. Iran’s nuclear capability is another, the second person said.

The U.S. asked Iran during the marathon talks in Islamabad for a 20-year suspension of uranium enrichment, the second person said. Iran agreed to three to five years, which Trump has said is not acceptable, the second person said.

The U.S. has also asked Iran to remove highly enriched uranium from the country, but Iran agreed to a “monitored process of down blending" — which is a process by which more dangerous, highly-enriched uranium is mixed with natural or less potent uranium to create a less potent material, according to the second person familiar with the ongoing negotiations.
A U.S. official told NBC News that there is continued engagement between the U.S. and Iran and forward motion on trying to get to an agreement.

The White House has a lot riding on the success of the diplomatic talks as polls show American voters souring on the conflict with Iran as inflation grinds on and gas prices rise. The president’s political advisers are looking ahead to the midterm elections in November, when Republicans will be fighting to preserve their razor-thin margin in the House and maintain their advantage in the Senate.
On Tuesday, Israel and Lebanon were slated to hold direct talks in Washington, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio also participating, according to a State Department official.
The high-level talks between Israel and Lebanon are the first since 1993, the official said.

The Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah fired rockets at Israel in early March in solidarity with Iran after the U.S. and Israel attacked the Islamic Republic on Feb. 28. And Israel has retaliated with attacks across Lebanon that have killed more than 2,000 people.

“This conversation will scope the ongoing dialogue about how to ensure the long-term security of Israel’s northern border and to support the Government of Lebanon’s determination to reclaim full sovereignty over its territory and political life,” the State Department official said. “Israel is at war with Hizballah, not Lebanon, so there is no reason the two neighbors should not be talking.”
In an interview with Fox News on Monday, Vance said there had been some "good conversations" with Tehran during the talks in the Pakistani capital.
Asked whether there might be more negotiations on the horizon, he said the question would be “best put to the Iranians, because the ball really is in their court.”
He added that there was a "grand deal to be had," but it was up to Tehran to "take the next step."

Trump said that the U.S. had been called “by the right people, the appropriate people, and they want to work a deal.” He said a day earlier that he didn't "care" whether Iran returned to the negotiating table.

"Despite all of the bluster, both sides would like to go down the escalation ladder,” Ross Harrison, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute and the author of “Decoding Iran’s Foreign Policy,” said in a phone interview Tuesday. “ I don’t think anybody relishes escalating further.”

“There’s a recognition that despite all the posturing, both on the ground with the blockade and also with some of the war of words, there’s a recognition that the only way really to resolve this is through negotiation,” he said.
Vance said that the key sticking point in the talks centered around Iran's enrichment of uranium.
"We must have the enriched material out of Iran," he said, adding: "We must have their conclusive commitment to not develop a nuclear weapon."
Vance said Tehran had shown some flexibility in talks over the weekend, but “didn’t move far enough.”

With the possibility of fresh talks on the horizon, Vance said, “if the Iranians are willing to meet us there, then this can be a very, very good deal for both countries. If they’re not willing to meet us there, that’s up to them.”
The International Atomic Energy Agency, the United Nations nuclear watchdog, has estimated that Iran has nearly 1,000 pounds of highly enriched uranium, enough for 11 nuclear weapons.

Tehran has maintained that its highly enriched uranium is still buried underground after U.S. airstrikes targeting enrichment facilities last year. It insists its nuclear program is peaceful and that it has no intention of seeking a nuclear weapon.
Iran’s armed forces accused the U.S. of “piracy” with the blockade and threatened ports across the Gulf if its own were hit.
But despite the risks of an intensifying standoff over Hormuz, the two-week ceasefire in the conflict seemed to be holding.
Israel and Lebanon, meanwhile, were holding rare direct talks in Washington on Tuesday in an effort to defuse another flashpoint that threatened the deal.
Israel has invaded southern Lebanon and attacked its neighbor from the air, including an intense day of deadly strikes on Beirut last week.
The Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah, which launched attacks on Israel last month in solidarity with Tehran, urged the Lebanese government to abandon the talks.
The U.S. and Israel have insisted Lebanon is not part of the ceasefire, but Iran and mediator Pakistan have said it is. Secretary of State Marco Rubio will join the talks in Washington on Tuesday.

title: Europe celebrates as Viktor Orbán's stunning defeat deals a blow to Putin and Trump
summary: Leaders across Europe hailed the end of Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule in Hungary and victory of Peter Magyar's center-right Tisza Party as a blow to Russia's Vladimir Putin
story: LONDON — “Europe! Europe!” was the chant ringing out from the banks of the Danube after an election upset that was celebrated across the continent Monday as a stunning defeat for those threatening its security.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

Presidents, prime ministers and European Union officials hailed the end of Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule in Hungary as a return to unity and shared values in the European Union, the 27-nation bloc that he criticized and sought to undermine.

Orbán, a close ally of Russia's Vladimir Putin and President Donald Trump, is viewed as a trailblazer for the global hard right. He conceded defeat Sunday night in what turned out to be a sweeping victory for challenger Peter Magyar and the center-right Tisza Party, which successfully united the country's opposition behind a vow to restore ties with Europe.
Why Hungary's election matters far beyond its borders
Tisza was set to win 138 seats in the 199-seat parliament, with almost all votes counted.
At a news conference Monday, Magyar offered pragmatic support for the United Nations and the European Union, a huge shift in Hungary's position toward Brussels.

"We are not going there to fight for the sake of fighting so we can write on billboards that Brussels is evil and needs to be stopped," he told supporters in a direct swipe at Orbán's negative campaigning, which also painted Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelenskyy as a potential enemy.

Magyar, who added that it was clear that Ukraine was the "victim" in the war, also accused the outgoing government of shredding documents relating to sanctions against Russia, citing an "insider" at the Foreign Affairs Ministry.
A succession of leaders called Magyar to offer their congratulations even before he took to the stage to address large crowds in the capital, Budapest, to declare victory.

Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk wrote on X: “Hungary, Poland, Europe, Back together! Glorious victory, dear friends!” He added “Ruszkik haza,” meaning “Russians go home” in Hungarian, a reference to Orbán's backing for Putin and repeated veto on European support for Ukraine against Russia's invasion.
In a video clip posted by Tusk, the clearly delighted Polish leader is seen speaking to Magyar by phone and says, "I think I'm happier than you."
Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez spoke for many on the continent when he posted: "Today Europe wins and European values win."

The European Democratic Party, a pan-European centrist group that supports greater integration across the continent, called Magyar's victory "a cry that shakes the conscience of us all" and posted a clip of crowds chanting "Europe! Europe!" at his victory rally in Budapest.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain — no longer a member of the E.U. but a close supporter of Ukraine — said: "This is an historic moment, not only for Hungary, but for European democracy."
The Kremlin, for its part, said Monday it hoped to continue its "pragmatic" ties with Hungary. “The Hungarians have made their choice. We respect this choice," spokesman Dmitry Peskov said.
But asked later whether the Kremlin would congratulate Magyar, Peskov said: “We don’t extend greetings to unfriendly countries. And Hungary is an unfriendly country."
Those celebrating the fall of Orbán should be mindful, analysts cautioned, that the huge influence he and his supporters built across the country’s institutions will not be easily removed.

“The structure of the last 16 years has been so entrenched and so much concerned with capturing all the commanding heights of the Hungarian state that it will take quite some time for anyone to unravel this,” said Jonathan Eyal, associated director of the Royal United Services Institute, a think tank in London.
Orbán, 62, is a key European ally of Trump whose populism and culture war posturing has provided a model for the MAGA movement.

In two decades at the helm of the Fidesz Party, he has staked out a reputation as a committed anti-immigration Christian nationalist who spoke of defending the West against what he framed as radical Islam and globalist forces.

The now former Hungarian leader has been widely criticized for dismantling or impairing many democratic institutions including the Constitution, law courts and the media, and packing influential committees with loyalists.

Tizsa is expected to have won at least two-thirds of the Hungarian parliament in the election, giving it a supermajority that means it can amend the Hungarian Constitution. "We will restore the system of checks and balances," Magyar told celebrating supporters Sunday.
He has vowed to unlock billions of dollars of in funding from the E.U. that had been halted due to repeated infringements and concerns about corruption and the democratic process.

But there are a series of official bodies stuffed with Orbán loyalists who have taken power away from Hungary's parliament to make policy, Eyal pointed out, as well as a powerful constitutional court full of his appointees.
"So sweeping away all these cobwebs is not something that could happen after a party on the Danube River," Eyal warned.
Magyar, a relatively young leader at 45, is a former member of Orbán's Fidesz Party and a former official in the Foreign Affairs Ministry — he only broke away to join Tisza in 2024.

Magyar is a traditionalist who support the hardline stance of the previous administration on immigration and has even signaled he may go further, by scrapping the country's guest worker scheme for non-E.U. citizens.

"Magyar is not exactly a dissident that has emerged out of pure thin air into the new leader," Eyal said. "So there is a legitimate question about how much of a break this is from someone who clearly shared some of the Orbán government's views for a lot of his political career."

And there is unlikely to be a complete severing of ties with Moscow. At his news conference Monday, Magyar said he hoped the war in Ukraine would soon end so international sanctions on Russia can be lifted — Hungary is a major importer of Russian oil. Hungary will try to diversify, he said, but he added that the country can't "change geography."
Whatever happens, this result is blow to the regime in Moscow, Eyal added.
"Most Hungarian voters when they're asked, they say we don't want to be part of Russia, we want to be part of Europe," he said. "And so this is a very major lesson for far right."

title: Oil prices might be starting to come down for a worrisome reason
summary: Oil prices have started to slip — but not necessarily for reasons that suggest a return to market normalcy
story: Oil prices have started to slip — but not necessarily for reasons that suggest a return to market normalcy.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

The International Energy Agency said Tuesday that “demand destruction” has begun to unfold. As a result of the acute energy commodity shortages stemming from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, oil appears to have reached a point where it is now so expensive that overseas businesses and households have begun curbing investment and consumption.

Countries in Asia, Europe and even other parts of the Middle East that depend on supplies passing through the strait have begun curtailing their use of natural gas, seen waves of flight cancellations and implemented policies to reduce overall fuel use, the agency noted in the report.
It’s a phenomenon likely to affect global economic growth. And while it does not yet appear to be affecting the U.S. economy, any impact would threaten to destabilize an already fragile labor market.
The IEA believes demand destruction could become a global trend.
“Demand destruction will spread as scarcity and higher prices persist,” it said.

The report comes as President Donald Trump has announced a targeted blockade of the Strait of Hormuz in a bid to increase economic pressure on Iran. The global economy has so far proven resilient — but that may soon change.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google

Traders have begun pricing in the new dynamic. The international price of a barrel has fallen to less than $98 after rising to as much as $118, while U.S. crude has fallen to $95 a barrel after hitting approximately $113 earlier this month.
U.S. gasoline prices have also begun to show slight declines from recent highs, according to AAA data.
While some of the decline is also due to hopes that the ceasefire announced last week will hold, Trump’s blockade is likely also playing a role.
As supply shortages become more acute, demand by definition will have to decline.
“Resuming flows through the Strait of Hormuz remains the single most important variable in easing the pressure on energy supplies, prices and the global economy,” the IEA said.

In a note to clients published March 31, Joseph Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM consultancy, discussed the lasting harm demand destruction can have on the economy — and why restrictions through the strait of other key industrial inputs besides crude oil, whose prices are also spiking, will also play a role.

“It means fewer cars sold, fewer homes bought, fewer restaurant meals, fewer business investments, and eventually fewer jobs,” he wrote. “And because the Strait of Hormuz crisis isn’t just about oil, the demand destruction this time could reach further than any standard model would predict.”

Thanks to changes in the economy since the 1970s, the potential impact on U.S. consumers may not be as great compared with other regions, he said. More energy efficient vehicles and work from home means the U.S. economy uses about half as much energy per dollar of gross domestic product as it did in 1980, he said. Plus, the U.S. is now a net oil producer.
“There is a real buffer,” he wrote.
On its latest earnings call Tuesday, J.P. Morgan executives said they had not yet seen U.S. consumers making significant changes in consumption as a result of higher oil prices.
"Tt’s not nothing, but it’s not overwhelming," chief financial officer Jeremy Barnum said.
But Brusuelas warned the U.S. would not be out of the woods in a prolonged-conflict scenario.

“None of these buffers have ever been tested against a disruption this large, hitting this many commodities at once,” he wrote. “If the strait stays closed past the summer, the probability of a recession would most likely be higher than 50%.”

title: Graphic: Tracking ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz
summary: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has been at a near-standstill for weeks amid the Iran war, sending prices of oil and other key goods soaring.
story: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has been at a near-standstill for weeks amid the Iran war, sending prices of oil and other key goods soaring.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
President Donald Trump made reopening the strait a condition of the ceasefire with Tehran, but with little change and no deal reached in peace talks, he announced a naval blockade of Iran.
NBC News is tracking the daily count of how many ships pass through the strait. Note that exact numbers may be higher; some ships manipulate their GPS trackers during transit.

title: Graphic: Track U.S. and state gas prices
summary: Are gas prices declining yet? NBC News is tracking national and state prices at the pump.
story: The Iran war has led to higher prices at the gas pump.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
Gas prices in the United States, up more than 30% since the U.S. and Israel launched the war, have soared as the global oil supply constricts.
Now at a national average of more than $4 per gallon, according to data from motor club AAA, this price increase is straining already-stretched budgets in households across the country.
NBC News is tracking gas prices and how they change at the national and state levels, and will be updating this article regularly with the latest data.

title: JD Vance tries to boost ailing MAGA ally ahead of Hungary election
summary: Vice President JD Vance repeatedly said he didn’t come to Hungary to tell people how to vote in their looming election.
story: BUDAPEST, Hungary — Vice President JD Vance repeatedly said he didn’t come to Hungary to tell people how to vote in their looming election.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
That changed during the barnstorming finale of his speech in Budapest late Tuesday.

“Will you stand for sovereignty and democracy, for truth and for the God of our forefathers?” Vance asked the Hungarian voters, cheered on by a standing ovation and whoops reverberating around the city’s MTK Sportpark arena. “Then, my friends, go to the polls this weekend, stand with Viktor Orbán, because he stands for you, and he stands for all these things.”

Vance flew to Hungary ostensibly to celebrate a “Hungarian-American friendship day.” In effect, he was the star attraction at a rally for Orbán, the Hungarian prime minister and a MAGA favorite, who is trailing in the polls days before Sunday’s nationwide election.

Orbán’s challenger is Peter Magyar, who was once a member of Orbán’s Fidesz party but now accuses him of rampant corruption. If polls are accurate, Magyar’s Tisza party is poised to assume power in the parliamentary vote.
Taking a break from talks to end the Iran war, Vance arrived in the midst of a febrile campaign. He said the U.S. would work with whoever wins before he joked that he knew Orbán would prevail anyway.

“We have got to get Viktor Orbán re-elected as prime minister of Hungary, don’t we?” Vance asked. Earlier, he had accused the European Union of “foreign election interference,” referring to the E.U.’s freezing of billions of euros in funding over Hungary’s democratic backsliding.
The irony was not lost on some observers.
“He is so openly campaigning for Orbán at this point that it cannot be seen as a regular state visit,” said Samuel Barczy, 27, one of a handful of protesters outside Vance’s speech.

“As you can see there’s not many demonstrators,” he told NBC News near Sandor Palace, the neoclassical presidential residence where Vance and Orbán held their earlier news conference. “But that’s because not many Hungarians know who Vance is.”

Indeed, it is highly unusual for a senior U.S. politician — or any figure from a Western country — to fly into the final days of a foreign election campaign to boost an ailing ally. E.U. spokesman Thomas Regnier responded to Vance’s visit and criticism by saying in a statement that “in Europe, elections are the sole choice of the citizens.”
Vance’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. On Wednesday, he defended himself against allegations of foreign interference at an event in Budapest.

"Foreign influence is when other governments threaten, cajole and try to use economic influence to tell you how to vote," he said. "Whether you like Viktor Orbán or not, whether you agree with this or that policy, that is fundamentally an assault on your sovereignty."

He is far from the only right-wing figure trying to keep their pioneer in power. An Orbán video this year showed a compilation of endorsements from the likes of Israel’s Benjamin Netanyahu, Argentina’s Javier Milei and France’s Marine le Pen.

Orbán has claimed “deep involvement” in crafting President Donald Trump’s message and political strategy. But more than that, he is a trailblazer for the international populist far right, railing against immigration, Muslims, the LGBTQ community and green policies. If anything, he plays into that image as the bête noire of mainstream politicians, branding his administration as an “illiberal democracy.”

The E.U. and many independent scholars say Hungary has moved beyond even that. The bloc says that Orbán’s Hungary can no longer be considered a full democracy but rather that it is an “elective autocracy,” having curbed judicial independence and press freedom. The country is also ranked as the most corrupt in Europe.

Orbán and his supporters deny the characterization that Hungary has become less democratic, pointing to the close electoral race as evidence. Orbán characterizes such foreign commentary on his country as an attempt to interfere in its domestic politics; he told the crowd Tuesday, “I recommend everyone keep their hands off Hungary.”
He also cast the election in apocalyptic terms.

On Tuesday, he said it was not just a vote about his political future; he called on “freedom-loving Americans and Hungarians” to “unite and save Western civilization.” He announced that Hungary would launch a “reconquista” of European institutions — referring to a 700-year military campaign by Catholic states to recapture the Iberian Peninsula from Muslim rule.
For Fidesz and Orbán, the stakes are high.

If Magyar’s party gets a two-thirds supermajority, it could launch “investigations into the Orbán government’s corruption,” the Atlantic Council think tank said in a briefing Monday. “Orbán’s response to an election loss would likely be defiant, and he would almost certainly demand a recount.” Indeed, he has echoed Trump’s false claims that he won the 2020 U.S. presidential election.
It has been a heated campaign, replete with allegations of Kremlin-inspired “false flag” operations, wiretapping and even a rumored sex tape involving Magyar himself.

But while Vance’s intervention provided another dramatic moment, it may not have a material impact on the result, according to Gábor Halmai, an emeritus professor at Hungary’s Eötvös Loránd University. Halmai said that there was no guarantee that Vance’s arrival would help Orbán’s electoral chances — and that it could even hinder them.

Trump polls higher in Hungary than in many other countries. But even here he remains a divisive figure, with 46% of people having little or no confidence in him, according to a poll by the Pew Research Center in June.
“I don’t know whether this kind of support will be positive for Orbán,” Halmai said. “Trump is not that popular among a lot of people.”
Orbán also has a complex and often fraught relationship with the E.U.

Hungary is one of the bloc’s 27 members, but Orbán has often been a thorn from within. Last month he vetoed a major 90 billion-euro ($104 billion) loan to help Ukraine weather Russia’s ongoing assault.

Many independent geopolitical analysts, as well as critics, point to Orbán’s close relationship with Russian President Vladimir Putin as a reason for his spoiler tactics. Orbán denies that, saying he is merely acting in Hungary’s own national interest and trying to stay out of the war.

Whatever the motives, Orbán, Putin and Vance often echo one another in their hostile tone toward the E.U. and its continued support of Ukraine. On Tuesday, Vance derided the “disgraceful,” “faceless bureaucrats” over their opposition to Orbán.
Inside the arena, supporters lapped it up.

“It meant so much that he spoke for so long, and also that he spoke from the heart — this was not a propaganda speech,” said Peter Kintsly, 67, an entrepreneur from Budapest, sharing a scarce taxi back into Budapest’s downtown.

Vance talked about the Hungarian battle against Soviet rule, including the failed, bloody revolution of 1956. He even invoked King Saint Stephen, the country’s first monarch, who ruled from 997 to 1001.
“He clearly knew what he was talking about,” Kintsly said.

Vance began by calling Trump and putting him on speakerphone. “I love that Viktor,” Trump told the crowd. “He didn’t allow people to storm your country and invade your country like other people have ruined their countries.”
From there, over the course of 40 minutes, Vance wove a story that had Washington and Budapest as the central players in a growing worldwide “revolution” against liberal elites.

“I see the way those same leaders sneer at the normal God-fearing people of Hungary,” he said. “I see that those who hate Europe the most, who hate its borders, its energy independence, the people who hate its Christian heritage, they hate one man above all others, and his name is Viktor Orbán.”

Vance told the crowd he wanted to discuss the “shared threat from within that both of our nations face. I speak, of course, of a far-left ideology given quarter in university circles, in the media and in our entertainment industry and increasingly among bureaucrats on both sides of the Atlantic.”
Earlier in the day, he called Orbán “one of the only true statesmen in Europe” — a caustic aside that will be heard in London, Berlin and Paris. “The president loves you,” Vance told him.

title: What to know about Trump's Iran blockade and the Strait of Hormuz
summary: The United States’ plan to blockade Iranian ports and marine traffic has sent oil prices surging, as the countries reignited their tussle over the crucial Strait of Hormuz after peace talks ended without a deal
story: The United States’ plan to blockade Iranian ports and marine traffic has sent oil prices surging, as the countries reignited their tussle over the crucial Strait of Hormuz after peace talks ended without a deal.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

American forces began enforcing the blockade at 10 a.m. ET Monday after President Donald Trump vowed to block “any and all Ships from trying to enter, or leave” the strait, which he had spent weeks pressuring Tehran to reopen.

The current two-week ceasefire came into force after Trump threatened to obliterate the “whole civilization” of Iran if it did not reopen the waterway, through which some 20% of the world’s oil passes.

The announcement of the blockade sent stocks falling and the price of crude oil soaring to above $100 amid questions around why the U.S. might cut off a trade route it had been trying to force open, how the blockade would work and what it could mean for countries across the world.
Add NBC News to Google
Add NBC News to Google
How will it work?

U.S. Central Command, or CENTCOM, clarified Trump’s Sunday comments, saying the blockade would affect only maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports. It stressed that U.S. forces would not “impede freedom of navigation” for vessels transiting the strait to and from other ports.

CENTCOM said the blockade on the key trade route would be enforced “impartially” against any and all vessels entering or departing Iranian ports, including those on the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman.

title: Viktor Orbán concedes election defeat in a blow to Hungary's far right
summary: Peter Magyar's center-right Tisza party is projected to win Hungary's parliamentary election, bringing an end to 16 years of authoritarian rule under Viktor Orbán, a far-right ally of both President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
story: BUDAPEST, Hungary — Viktor Orbán conceded defeat Sunday after 16 years in power, as Peter Magyar’s center-right Tisza Party was projected to win Hungary’s parliamentary election.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
With almost all of the ballots tallied, Tisza is projected to win 138 seats in the 199-seat legislature, Reuters reported, enough for a supermajority.
Speaking to supporters in Budapest, Orbán said the result was "clear" and "painful" for his party.
“The responsibility and possibility of governing was not given to us,” he said.
He added that he had congratulated the winning party and that his party, Fidesz, would serve Hungary as the opposition. Magyar said on Facebook that Orbán had congratulated him.
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen hailed Tisza’s landslide victory as a move by the country toward Europe.
“Europe’s heart is beating stronger in Hungary tonight,” von der Leyen said on X. “Hungary has chosen Europe. A country reclaims its European path. The Union grows stronger.”

title: After collapse of U.S.-Iran talks in Pakistan, the path to peace is less clear
summary: Vice President JD Vance and Iranian officials both said significant differences remain in the quest for a deal that could bring a more permanent end to the war, though the fragile two-week ceasefire remains in place.
story: A day of talks, one “final” offer, no resolution.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.
Negotiators from Washington and Tehran left talks in Pakistan on Sunday with little to show for it and a return to fighting between the two sides looming large.

Vice President JD Vance and Iranian officials both said significant differences remain in the quest for a deal that could bring a more permanent end to the war, though the fragile two-week ceasefire remains in place.

“We were negotiating in good faith,” Vance said at a press conference. “We leave here with a very simple proposal, a method of understanding that is our final and best offer. We’ll see if the Iranians accept it.”

With just eight days left in the current ceasefire, the failure to find a more permanent agreement in Pakistan raises the prospect of a return to a war that saw President Donald Trump threaten to wipe out an entire civilization, with the death toll already in the thousands and the impacts rippling through the global economy.
The U.S. failed to gain Iran’s trust during negotiations in Islamabad, Iran’s parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, who led the delegation in Pakistan, said Sunday.

In a post on X, Ghalibaf said his colleagues presented “forward-moving initiatives” but the U.S. was unable “to gain the trust of the Iranian delegation,” pointing to a history of failed talks and agreements.
Iranian state media said “excessive demands” sank the possibility of a deal.

The sides could not find common ground on a number of key matters, including the Strait of Hormuz and the country’s development of nuclear technology, Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting said on Telegram.
Key among the differences in Pakistan was whether Iran would commit to not seeking to develop a nuclear weapon, Vance said.
Trump and Hegseth lean into religious framing of Iran war

“We haven’t seen that yet. We hope that we will,” he said. A past deal to limit Iran’s nuclear ambitions in exchange for lifting economic sanctions was negotiated under Barack Obama in 2015, but the first Trump administration ripped up that deal in 2018.
Trump said Sunday that the meeting went well and that "most points were agreed to."
"But the only point that really mattered, NUCLEAR, was not," he wrote on Truth Social.
Pakistan, whose government mediated the U.S.-Iran talks, has urged the U.S. and Iran to continue their ceasefire despite peace talks in Islamabad concluding with no agreement to end the war.

In a post on X, Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar characterized the 21-hour summit as both intense and constructive, saying the nations should maintain the talks’ “positive spirit,” move forward with the goal of regionwide “peace and prosperity” and vow to continue to withhold attacks against each other.
“It is imperative that the parties continue to uphold their commitment to ceasefire,” he said.
As that takes place, he said, Pakistan will stand by.
Iranian officials have not released a recent death toll, but the U.S.-based rights group HRANA put the total number of people killed in Iran at almost 3,400, including more than 1,600 civilians.
Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway through which 20% of the world’s oil passed before the war, remains at a virtual standstill and oil prices continue to rise.

U.S. Central Command said U.S. warships are setting conditions to reopen a safe passage after Iran laid mines in the key shipping lane. Meanwhile, Trump said the U.S. would impose its own blockade on the strait, accusing Iran of trying to extort vessels for passage.

“Effective immediately, the United States Navy, the Finest in the World, will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz," he wrote on Truth Social. In a second post, he again accused Iran of seeking to block the Strait, despite saying he would blockade it himself.

But with Iran retaining leverage over the strait and the risk to tankers unresolved, attention turns to what comes next. Analysts say the failure in Islamabad reflects deeper, long-standing divisions that go far beyond a single round of talks.
“It is no surprise that the U.S.-Iran talks did not end in a diplomatic breakthrough,” Fawaz Gerges, a professor of international relations at the London School of Economics, told NBC News.

The Iranian side sees the Strait of Hormuz as “their most potent weapon,” he added, while the Americans want it to “open now.” A U.S. insistence that Tehran limit uranium enrichment and turn over its current stockpile, meanwhile, is a “nonstarter” for the Iranians, he said.

Vance and his team went into the negotiating session really asking the Iranians “to capitulate,” Gerges said. “The Iranians will say, ‘You haven’t defeated Iran. We will not surrender, we want to bargain, we want to negotiate, because we have asserted our control over the Strait of Hormuz.’”

Danny Citrinowicz, a senior researcher on Iran at the Institute for National Security Studies, which is affiliated with Tel Aviv University in Israel, said that both sides had come to the table with the assumption they had won.
“It’s not that the Iranians want escalation, but I think that for them, they prefer escalation to dictation,” he said. “They think that they have the upper hand.”
While Vance said the “final” U.S. offer was on the table, Citrinowicz said that there is “no doubt behind the scenes mediators are trying to reach common ground.”
“You cannot, after 21 hours, say it’s all over,” he said. “These are complex issues.”
The 2015 nuclear agreement took almost two years to be finalized, Gerges noted, and that was not sealed during a war. “I think there’s a long way to go,” he said.

title: Cuba's president says 'we would die' to defend against U.S. invasion
summary: In an exclusive interview with NBC News, Miguel Díaz-Canel stressed the need for dialogue but said Cuba is prepared to defend itself.
story: President Miguel Díaz-Canel stood by Cuba’s leadership and didn’t concede a need for any changes to its government amid President Donald Trump’s pressure campaign against the communist country.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

In a wide-ranging interview with NBC News’ “Meet the Press” moderator Kristen Welker in Havana on Thursday, Díaz-Canel said there’s no “justification for the United States to launch a military aggression against Cuba.”
“An invasion to Cuba would have costs. ... It would affect the security of Cuba, the United States and of the region,” he said through a translator in his first American broadcast interview.

“If that happens, there will be fighting, and there will be a struggle, and we will defend ourselves, and if we need to die, we’ll die, because as our national anthem says, ‘Dying for the homeland is to live,’” the Cuban president said.

“Before making that decision, which is so irrational, there is a logic, that is, the logic of dialogue, to engage in discussions, to debate and try to reach agreements that would move us away from confrontation,” Díaz-Canel said.
Kristen Welker reports on the ground from Havana, Cuba

Welker asked Díaz-Canel whether he was willing to commit to responding to “key demands” from the U.S., including releasing political prisoners, scheduling multiparty elections and recognizing unions and a free press.

“Nobody has made those demands to us, and we have established that in respect to our political system or constitutional order, these are issues that are not under negotiations with the United States,” Díaz-Canel answered, adding that those issues are “extensively manipulated.”

Welker then pressed Díaz-Canel on the issue of political prisoners, asking whether Cuba would commit to their release and specifically naming Cuban rapper Maykel Osorbo, a Latin Grammy winner who has been in prison since 2021 for writing a protest song after thousands of Cubans took to the streets to protest conditions and shortages during the Covid pandemic.

Díaz-Canel did not commit to releasing political prisoners and rejected their characterization as such, saying there are people in Cuba who are not in favor of the revolution “and manifest themselves on a daily basis” who are not in prison.

“This narrative that has been created, that image that anyone who speaks against a revolution is thrown into jail, that’s a big lie, that’s a slander, and that’s part of that construct in order to vilify and to engage a character assassination of the Cuban Revolution,” Díaz-Canel said.
International human rights organizations like PEN International and Amnesty International have called for Osorbo’s release.

There is evidence that the Trump administration’s pressure campaign has been felt in the country. Around mid-March, Cuban officials took a sharp turn in their tone toward the U.S., saying they are prepared to confront any attacks from the U.S. The government ordered an increase in military exercises that often air during national newscasts.
Díaz-Canel has insisted Cuba’s position is “entirely defensive and not aggressive,” and he’s made similar remarks in recent speeches and interviews.
“Again, let me repeat. This is not what we want. We don’t want war. We don’t want an attack,” he said.
Díaz-Canel urged a dialogue based on “respect” between the two countries’ forms of government.
Welker asked if it was possible to “get a deal with President Trump.”
“I think dialogue and deals with the U.S. government are possible,” Díaz-Canel answered, “but they’re difficult.” He said he had not spoken to Secretary of State Marco Rubio and he doesn’t know him.

Trump said last month when asked about Cuba that there may be “a friendly takeover, it may not be a friendly takeover.” Rubio told reporters on March 27, “You have to change the people in charge,” adding the country was a “disaster” because its economic system didn’t work.
